title: Makefile: Remove broken targets
body:
The configuration for this container has moved to the
containers/automation_images repository.  Leverage the
new location to guarantee synchronization with containers
used in CI.

Signed-off-by: Chris Evich <cevich@redhat.com>

comments.body: Note to me: There is no `quay.io/libpod/fedora_podman:latest`, would need to update automation to push one (easy task)
Is there any value in making the automation repo a submodule of Podman if we need to check it out here?

>  I'm going to need a whole lot more context to understand this.

@edsantiago context: "Hey cevich! make dbuild is broken" - that's all I got :confused: Looking at the Makefile, it seems several targets try to use the locally built image. Perhaps a secondary-problem:  I'm not aware of any automated tests that check these targets.

> Is there any way to build this image from the automation_images repo instead? (Is that what your recent comment suggests?)

Yes and yes.  Basically I just need to make the build scripting push a `:latest` image if executing against a branch.  Or, point quay.io's auto-builder service at it.  Another idea would be to parse out the `_BUILT_IMAGE_SUFFIX` value from `.cirrus.yml` and use that as the image tag (this would be more synchronized tag than `latest`).

> really uncomfortable about running a git checkout within a git checkout.

Me too, and @mheon idea of a submodule would basically fit the same bill.
Clarification: "Another idea would be to parse out the"...from a Makefile target or script.

Okay, here's the latest idea: Slurp out the tag from `.cirrus.yml` and use the pre-built image.  This seems to work (I tested `make shell`) but the image isn't a very human-friendly size (i.e. it's huge).

Poking further...a lot of the make targets that use `${LIBPOD_IMAGE}` are horribly broken for use on Fedora (esp. the volume-mounts). They assume you're running as root and always disable SELInux :confounded: 

In fact, I'm not sure how they ever functioned as intended...which begs the question: @mheon can we simply delete these targets :grin:   That would be the easiest solution of all :smiley:

At least one person is using them, but I agree that we need to clean them up. I think the SELinux thing is normal/expected (we can't reasonably expect to relabel the directory the user is building out of) but the rest needs to be fixed so it actually works.

> At least one person is using them

@mheon are you sure?  Near as I can tell, none of these targets can or did work as intended, for a very long time.  Besides SELinux, none of them specify a working directory, and so the repository volume-mount is completely useless.  The container will try to run 'make' from `/`.  Even the previous `Dockerfile` specified a working-dir different from the volume-mount (I just checked).  

If it's only one person...can I/we ask them if it's okay to delete:

* `dbuild*`
* `test`
* `integration`
* `shell`
* `testunit`

?
`dbuild` dates back to a031b83, November 2017, "Initial checkin from CRI-O repo". It has had only the most trivial of edits since then. How sure are we that someone is using this?

Update: I killed off `dbuild*` and kinda-sorta mostly fixed the other targets.  The unittests run and pass, but integration fails.  

@edsantiago WDYT about my calling a python script from Makefile to parse `.cirrus.yml`?  I'm slightly nervous because it assumes python3 and python3-pyyaml are both installed.  However, the alternative of parsing YAML with bash or grep/cut seems even more fugly.  I think this is a preferable solution to using 'latest' - since that's a very ambiguous tag (never mind it not currently existing).
@edsantiago Pretty sure we have at least one user, because someone asked me about it not working this morning in Google Chat

@cevich if this is something that runs in CI, we can guarantee having `python3` and any required modules. If this isn't in CI, well, I still think we can assume that the entire known universe has python3 by now.

@mheon excellent, that means we can identify that party and bring them into this conversation. Out of curiosity, do you know if it has *ever* worked for them?

@edsantiago my guess is "no", these targets all look to me like they're intended for human-use.  Automation does things differently in each case:

* Build for darwin inside a container (kind of duplicates `podman-remote-darwin`)
* Build all binaries inside a container
* Run only integration tests inside a container
* Run only unit tests inside a container
* Run both unit and integration tests inside a container
* Drop into a shell inside a container

>my guess is "no",

I'm not sure I understand your response. Your guess about what?
@edsantiago Yes, though I understand that he had to make changes to the Makefile to get it working which became https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/7218

> I'm not sure I understand your response. Your guess about what?

These targets *do not* run in any automation that I am aware of, so I'm guessing they're intended only for human-consumption.

> get it working which became #7218

So...I can get these working by swapping `--privledged` for `--security-opt label=disable` and fixing the mount paths so the image's ``WORKDIR`` is used (`/var/tmp/go/.../podman`).  Where "working" means it does what I think was the original intention...on my Fedora 32 laptop.  I have no idea if that's relevant/important to the one user or not...which is why I think we should get rid of these.  They seem likely to easily break again in the future and continue to be a maintenance hassle.  Assuming it's really one user that cares, they could easily write their own bash aliases or small scripts which 100% reflect their own environment.  In other words, trying to have a generally-applicable solution here is always going to be an uphill-battle with lots and lots of assumptions.

My takeaway:
* podman team does not use these
* podman team doesn't even know anything about these

Therefore:
* podman team should not maintain or support these
In anticipation of "Okay, let's delete them", I rebased against upstream and nuked these (and two more) broken targets.
LGTM but could you squash and update the PR title?
@mheon @edsantiago okay, this should be good to go.
@cevich it looks like this has been pending testing for 3 hours with no progress?

> @cevich it looks like this has been pending testing for 3 hours with no progress?

The title `[ci skip]` does what it says.  Here, I can do this to make it show green things...
Yep, if it makes you feel more comfortable :smile:
rebased and force-pushed

/approve
/lgtm
